statement: For the 2021–2028 period, the EEA EFTA states' combined financial contribution through the EEA Grants and Norway Grants mechanisms totals approximately €3.3 billion (of which Norway pays ~97%, Iceland ~2.5%, and Liechtenstein ~0.5%). Iceland's share amounts to roughly €10–12 million per year. By comparison, an EU membership contribution for Iceland, based on GNI-proportional calculations, has been estimated at €150–250 million per year gross (before accounting for EU funds received).
source_name: EEA Grants Financial Mechanism Committee; European Commission budget data
caveats: The EU membership contribution estimate is indicative and depends heavily on GNI fluctuations, negotiated corrections, and receipts from CAP, structural funds, and other EU programmes. Iceland's high GDP per capita would make it a net contributor, but the net position is uncertain.

related_entries.statement: Iceland currently contributes to the EEA/EFTA Grants mechanism, which provides approximately €180 million per year (shared among Iceland, Norway, and Liechtenstein) to reduce social and economic disparities in the EEA. Norway pays the vast majority. As an EU member, Iceland's estimated net budget contribution would be significantly higher, though the exact amount depends on GDP and negotiated rebates.

The EU has adopted a vast body of legislation since its founding. As of end 2024, the EUR-Lex database records approximately 146,700 regulations and 4,600 directives adopted since 1958, totalling over 151,000 binding legal acts. During the EEA period (1994–2024), the EU adopted roughly 57,100 regulations (averaging 1,842 per year) and 2,626 directives (85 per year). Legislative output has declined from a peak of approximately 4,000+ regulations per year in the late 1980s to around 1,200 per year in 2019–2023.
